summary: Gruber eviscerates a Washington Post story that characterizes Apple and Google's COVID-19 exposure notification system as 'practically useless,' arguing the reporters and their sources fundamentally misunderstand how the system works. He points out that the system stores data entirely on users' devices, not on Apple or Google's servers, and that the privacy protections critics call an 'unbending stance' are actually essential for public adoption. Gruber systematically dismantles quotes from academics, anti-monopoly advocates, and health officials who argue the companies should allow location tracking and automatic data sharing with government authorities. He notes the irony of sources claiming Apple and Google exercise 'sovereign power' when the companies are simply complying with existing privacy laws like GDPR. The piece highlights that the APIs hadn't even been released yet, making criticisms about real-world usefulness premature.
key_points: The exposure notification system stores data entirely on users' devices — Apple and Google never have access to it, contradicting the Post's framing
Privacy protections aren't an 'unbending stance' but table stakes for building a system people will actually install and use
The Post's sources appear not to have read Apple and Google's published FAQ or cryptography specifications explaining exactly how the system works
Automatically sharing location data with government officials would likely violate GDPR and similar privacy laws worldwide — the opposite of what the Post's sources advocate
The system hadn't even launched yet, making real-world effectiveness criticisms premature and unfounded
money_quotes: It's so atrociously bad — factually wrong and one-sided in opinion — that it should be retracted.

"Unbending stance" is a rather harsh description of Apple and Google's desire not to "undermine people's privacy" or "drain phone battery life". This isn't an "unbending stance". It's table stakes for designing a system that people will actually install and use.
Google and Apple will not "have the data". It is stored entirely and only on each user's own device. We, the users, will have the data, and we, the users, can share that data with our doctors.

And how in the world did "At least they're constrained by laws" make it into this story? Nissenbaum believes Apple and Google are not constrained by laws? That will be news to both companies' legal compliance departments, who I presume will soon be laid off.

Good luck finding public support for a system that turns phones into surveillance devices that report anything at all automatically to the government, let alone something as sensitive as who we've been in contact with and where we've been.
"Some officials" — unnamed, unsourced — are arguing that the government should enjoy "minute-by-minute location logs recorded by people's phones" and this is given zero pushback in a news story.
It is legit amazing to see Ashkan Soltani, of all people, say "we've overcompensated for privacy."
Maybe because they're not fucking out yet? Hallelujah, holy shit — where's the Tylenol?
key_insight: The Washington Post's sources fundamentally misunderstand that Apple and Google's exposure notification system keeps all data on users' devices, and the privacy protections they decry as obstructionism are both legally required and essential for public trust and adoption.
